As conscription, impressment, and tax-in-kind fell hardest on yeoman farmers while planters claimed exemptions, common people lost faith in the cause. The failure was the administration's failure to answer the grievances of its own people — nationalism that never took root cannot sustain a war.

perspectives.voice: Scholar — Emory Thomas, The Confederate Nation (1979)
perspectives.text: The cruel irony is that the Confederacy had to centralize — conscription, impressment, national taxation, government control of industry and railroads — to survive at all, becoming the very leviathan it had seceded to escape. It out-centralized the antebellum United States and still could not match the Union, and the centralizing wrecked its founding consensus in the process.

Governor Joseph Brown of Georgia denounced conscription to Davis's face as 'subversive of her [Georgia's] sovereignty,' a 'palpable violation of the Constitution,' and an 'usurpation' tending to 'military despotism' — and he shielded thousands by appointing them to state offices. Governor Zebulon Vance of North Carolina kept large state stores of uniforms, shoes, and blankets reserved for North Carolina troops, and his broad reading of the exemption laws sheltered more men from the draft than any other governor — even as North Carolina, in fact, also fed the Confederate army more conscripts and supplies than any other state, which complicates the simple 'hoarding' charge. The states' rights creed, aimed at the Union, turned inward against Richmond.

Alexander Stephens, the Confederacy's own Vice President, spent much of the war back home in Georgia leading the opposition — condemning conscription (in an anonymous 1862 newspaper letter) and, before the Georgia legislature in 1864, denouncing the suspension of habeas corpus as despotism. When the second-highest officer of the government wages a states'-rights war against his own administration, the dysfunction is not marginal; it is at the center.

perspectives.voice: Lost Cause myth — named and countered
perspectives.text: The Lost Cause held that a noble, united South was overwhelmed only by brute Northern numbers and resources — defeat as tragedy, never as failure. The record refutes it. The Confederacy was divided against itself: governors withholding troops, a vice president in open opposition, a War Office in permanent crisis, common people deserting an elite's war, and enslaved people fleeing to Union lines by the hundreds of thousands. The South was not betrayed by faint hearts or crushed by numbers alone; its government was hollowed out by the localism it was founded to defend.

perspectives.voice: Scholar — Frank Owsley, State Rights in the Confederacy (1925)
perspectives.text: The originating verdict, and the bluntest: the Confederacy 'died of states' rights.' Owsley's old thesis overstates a single cause and rests on a Lost-Cause-adjacent worldview, but it named the wound. Later scholars refined the mechanism — Davis's failures, weak nationalism, class fracture — without erasing the core insight that the South's own structure helped kill it.

perspectives.voice: Caution — Beringer, Hattaway, Jones & Still, Why the South Lost the Civil War (1986)
perspectives.text: Not all scholars accept that states' rights was decisive. These authors argue the obstruction was more rhetoric than substance — that the Confederacy did centralize effectively — and locate defeat instead in a fragile nationalism and a failing will to fight, sapped by guilt over slavery and military reverses. The debate is live; but every camp here agrees the cause was substantially internal, not merely the arithmetic of men and guns.

perspectives.voice: Synthesis — slavery at the root, and the enslaved as actors
perspectives.text: The governance dysfunction traces back to slavery. The states'-rights ideology that paralyzed Richmond existed to protect human bondage — Vice President Stephens himself, in the 1861 'Cornerstone Speech,' declared slavery and white supremacy the Confederacy's founding 'cornerstone.' The class fracture Escott describes pitted slaveholding planters, who won exemptions, against non-slaveholding yeomen, who bled. And the enslaved were not passive stakes in that quarrel: by fleeing to Union lines in the hundreds of thousands, resisting impressment, and ultimately taking up arms in U.S. uniform, they drained the Confederate labor and manpower base and forced the regime into the very coercive, centralizing measures that shattered its founding consensus. A republic built to perpetuate slavery could not cohere into a nation — its founding principle was the right of a local elite to resist any power, even its own, that threatened the institution — while the people it enslaved worked to bring it down. The contradiction was fatal.

sources: Rable, The Confederate Republic: A Revolution against Politics (1994)
Thomas, The Confederate Nation, 1861-1865 (1979)
Escott, After Secession: Jefferson Davis and the Failure of Confederate Nationalism (1978)
Owsley, State Rights in the Confederacy (1925)
Beringer, Hattaway, Jones & Still, Why the South Lost the Civil War (1986)
McPherson, Battle Cry of Freedom (1988)
Brown-Davis Conscription Correspondence (DocSouth, UNC; primary source)
Stephens, 'Cornerstone Speech' (Savannah, Mar. 21, 1861; primary source)
